title: Jones-Roberts solitary waves and the onset of rotation in a spherical surface condensate
authors: Noel Cuadra, Thomas Gasenzer, Davide Proment, Alberto Villois
abstract: The nonlinear excitations underlying the onset of rotation in a dilute Bose-Einstein condensate confined to a thin spherical shell are studied. These excitations correspond to solitary waves rotating about the sphere at constant angular speed: at low speeds they appear as dipoles of singly quantized vortices with opposite circulation, while at higher speeds they evolve into vortex-free Jones-Roberts solitons. With further increase of the angular speed, these excitations hybridize with equatorially confined modes whose azimuthal wave number is set by the sphere radius measured in units of the healing length. The propagation speed of these modes is shown to play the role of a Landau critical velocity, thereby setting the upper limiting angular speed of the entire Jones-Roberts family.
summary: This paper explores how the curvature and topology of a spherical surface influence the formation and stability of solitary waves in a Bose-Einstein condensate. It demonstrates that these waves evolve from vortex dipoles to vortex-free solitons as rotation speed increases, with a maximum speed dictated by the Landau critical velocity. The findings provide a bridge between classical Jones-Roberts solitons in flat planes and excitations in curved, topologically non-trivial geometries.
main_problem: The study investigates the nonlinear excitations (solitary waves) that drive the onset of rotation in a Bose-Einstein condensate confined to a thin spherical shell, specifically how curvature and topology affect these waves.
main_result: The authors identify a continuous family of excitations ranging from vortex dipoles to vortex-free Jones-Roberts solitons, and determine that the Landau critical velocity sets the upper limit for the angular speed of these waves.
method: The study uses the mean-field Gross-Pitaevskii equation solved via an iterative Newton-Raphson scheme with pseudo-spectral accuracy and branch-following continuation.
model_or_system: A dilute, quasi-two-dimensional Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) confined to a thin spherical shell of radius R, modeled by the Gross-Pitaevskii equation on a spherical surface.
key_observables: Density profile, phase angle, angular momentum (Lz), energy (E), and Bogoliubov excitation spectrum.
important_parameters: Sphere radius (R) in units of healing length, angular speed (Omega), chemical potential (mu), and the large-radius (Euclidean) limit.
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to be a dilute gas in the mean-field regime, confined to a thin shell with strong radial confinement, and the ground state density is assumed to be uniform.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic of the BEC on a spherical shell and visual representations of the density and phase angle for a specific vortex dipole configuration.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical system and GP model, describes the numerical Newton-Raphson and continuation methods, analyzes the different regimes of solitary waves (vortex dipoles to solitons), derives the energy-momentum relation, and establishes the stability condition via the Landau critical velocity.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and AMO physics as it explores how topological constraints and curved geometries modify fundamental nonlinear phenomena like solitons and the Landau criterion for superfluidity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. BEC localised within a thin spherical shell of radius R. (a) Angular coordinate system (ϕ, θ). Color-coded representation of (b) the density and (c) the phase angle of the complex field of a solitary wave, consisting of a dipole of two oppositely-charged vortices on a longitude, equidistant from the equator (marked by a thin grey line) and rotating with constant angular speed Ωabout the z-axis (here R = 10 and Ω≈0.030).

FIG. 2. The energies Esw of the solitary wave solutions for a given angular momentum Lsw for the branch with a radius R = 10 of the sphere in units of the healing length (blue curve); the dotted line in- dicates solutions above the critical angular velocity Ωcr, i.e., vortex- free Jones–Roberts-type solitons. The slope of the black dashed- dotted line equals the maximum angular speed ΩLandau. The insets show the density field for four different solutions along the branch: a vortex-free soliton, the solution at the critical Ωcr, a dipole, and the polar-vortex configuration where the vortices are sitting at the poles.

FIG. 3. Angular-momentum dependence of the solitary wave en- ergy for different dimensionless radii, R ∈{10, 20, 30}. The dot- ted lines indicate the region of vortex-free density dip solutions and the solid dots mark the polar-vortex solutions. The inset shows the same branches rescaled according to the Rankine-vortex energy and angular momentum estimates for the polar-vortex solutions where f(R/ξ) = 1 + 4 log(2R/ξ), see Eq. (8).

FIG. 4. (a) Fractions ωBog(l)/l for Bogoliubov frequencies (6), for two different values of the radius, R10, 20. As the angular- momentum quantum number l is integer-valued, only the dots repre- sent possible angular frequencies, cf. Eq. (9), while the dashed lines are guides to the eye. (b) Density fields of the solitary wave solution, in the limit of the Landau critical angular speed ΩLandau, for R = 10 (top) and R = 20 (bottom). The white bars indicate the wave angle 2π/lLandau in the θ-direction.
